revision.text:
[[commons:Category:Homi-jinja (Asahimachi, Nirasaki)]][[ja:穂見神社 (韮崎市旭町)]] {{Expand Japanese|穂見神社 (韮崎市旭町)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Homi Shrine (Asahimachi, Nirasaki) | native_name = 穂見神社 | image = Homi Shrine Nirasaki City.JPG | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Homi Shrine (Asahimachi, Nirasaki) | coordinates = {{coord|35.67311111|138.43725}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = {{ill|Amenotokotachi|lt=Amenotokotachi|fr|Amenotokotachi|ja|天之常立神|zh|天常立尊|qid=Q10939047|1=Ame-no-Tokotachi}} }} {{nihongo|'''Homi Shrine (Asahimachi, Nirasaki)'''|穂見神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Kai Province]]. == instance of (P31) == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|lt=Shinto shrine|ar|جينجا|arz|ضريح شنتو|bcl|Shinto shrine|ca|Jinja|cs|Šintoistická svatyně|da|Shinto-tempel|de|Shintō-Schrein|el|Σιντοϊστικό Ιερό|en|Shinto shrine|eo|Ŝintoa sanktejo|es|Jinja|eu|Jinja|fa|معبد شینتویی|fi|Šintolainen pyhäkkö|fr|Sanctuaire shinto|hi|शिंतो मंदिर|hr|Šintoistički hram|hu|Sintó szentély|id|Jinja|it|Jinja|ja|神社|ka|ძინძია|ko|신사 (신토)|lb|Shintō-Schräin|mr|शिंतो मंदिर|nl|Jinja (heiligdom)|no|Jinja|or|ଜିଞ୍ଜା|pl|Jinja|pt|Templo xintoísta|ru|Дзиндзя|sc|Jinja|simple|Shinto shrine|sl|Šintoistično svetišče|sr|Шинтоистички храм|sv|Jinja|th|ศาลเจ้าชินโต|tr|Şinto tapınağı|uk|Святилище (синто)|vi|Thần xã|wuu|神社|zh_yue|神社|zh|神社|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} * {{ill|Shikinai Ronsha|lt=Shikinai Ronsha|ja|論社|qid=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} * Province}} ** Series ordinal: 11 ** P155: {{ill|Kamuheno Shrine|lt=Kamuheno Shrine|qid=Q135039697|1=Kamuheno Shrine (Q135039697)}} ** P156: {{ill|Q111495856|ltq=Q111495856|qid=Q111495856|1=Uwato Shrine}} == located in the administrative territorial entity (P131) == * {{ill|Q290326|ltq=Q290326|qid=Q290326|1=Nirasaki, Yamanashi}} * {{ill|Kai Province|lt=Kai Province|ckb|پارێزگای کای|cs|Provincie Kai|de|Provinz Kai|en|Kai Province|eo|Provinco Kai|es|Provincia de Kai|eu|Kai probintzia|fa|ولایت کای|fi|Kain provinssi|fr|Province de Kai|gl|Provincia de Kai|id|Provinsi Kai|it|Kai (provincia)|ja|甲斐国|ko|가이국|lt|Kajaus žemė|ms|Wilayah Kai|nl|Kai (provincie)|pt|Província de Kai|ru|Каи (провинция)|simple|Kai Province|sk|Kai (provincia)|sr|Провинција Каи|tr|Kai (vilayet)|uk|Провінція Кай|wuu|甲斐国|zh_min_nan|Kai-kok|zh_yue|甲斐國|zh|甲斐國|qid=Q858076|1=Kai Province}} == coordinate location (P625) == * {{coord|35.67311111|138.43725}} == image (P18) == * Homi Shrine Nirasaki City.JPG == dedicated to (P825) == * {{ill|Q10939047|ltq=Q10939047|qid=Q10939047|1=Ame-no-Tokotachi}} == Kokugakuin University Digital Museum entry ID (P13677) == * 181666 ** P3831: {'time': '+1946-02-02T00:00:00Z', 'timezone': 0, 'before': 0, 'after': 0, 'precision': 11, 'calendarmodel': 'http://www.wikidata.org/entity/Q1985727'} ** P1534: {{ill|Q3029647|ltq=Q3029647|qid=Q3029647|1=Shinto Directive}} == country (P17) == * {{ill|Q17|ltq=Q17|qid=Q17|1=Japan}} == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 8090005004699 == Commons category (P373) == * Homi-jinja (Asahimachi, Nirasaki) == said to be the same as (P460) == * {{ill|Homino Shrine|lt=Homino Shrine|qid=Q135039702|1=Homino Shrine}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '山梨県韮崎市旭町上条南割', 'language': 'ja'} * {'text': '山梨県南アルプス市高尾', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Homi-jinja (Asahimachi, Nirasaki)}} [[JA:穂見神社 (韮崎市旭町)]] {{wikidata link|Q11597096}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Homi Shrine |画像 = [[File:Homi Shrine Nirasaki City.JPG|250px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|qid=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Nirasaki|Q290326|qid=Q290326|1=Nirasaki, Yamanashi}} Asahi-chō Kamijō Minamiwari 3388 |位置 = {{coord|35|40|23.2|N|138|26|14.1|E|region:JP-19|display=inline,title}}Location of the satomiya. |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Tokotachi-no-Kami|Q10939047|qid=Q10939047|lt=Ame-no-Sokotachi-no-Mikoto|1=Ame-no-Tokotachi}}
{{ill|qid=Q478504|lt=Kunitokotachi-no-Kami|Q478504|qid=Q478504|lt=Kuni-no-Tokotachi-no-Mikoto|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}}
{{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|qid=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Toyouke-hime}} |社格 = candidate shrine for {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}}
former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|qid=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |創建 = (trad.) {{ill|lt=Jinki|Q1373210|qid=Q1373210|1=Jinki (era)}} 1 ({{ill|lt=724|Q23549|qid=Q23549|1=724}}) |本殿 = |別名 = Nadeshikiyama Gongen |札所等 = |例祭 = Sunday nearest {{ill|lt=October 17|Q2962|qid=Q2962|1=October 17}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Homi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Homi Shrine''' (ほみじんじゃ) is a shrine in Kamijō Minamiwari, Asahi-chō, {{ill|lt=Nirasaki|Q290326|qid=Q290326|1=Nirasaki, Yamanashi}}, {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|qid=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}. It is a candidate shrine for a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} and was formerly a {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|qid=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. Also called Nadeshikiyama Gongen. The {{ill|lt=enshrined deities|Q11591100|qid=Q11591100|1=Saijin}} are {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Tokotachi-no-Kami|Q10939047|qid=Q10939047|lt=Ame-no-Sokotachi-no-Mikoto|1=Ame-no-Tokotachi}}, {{ill|qid=Q478504|lt=Kunitokotachi-no-Kami|Q478504|qid=Q478504|lt=Kuni-no-Tokotachi-no-Mikoto|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}}, and {{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|qid=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Toyouke-hime}}. ===History=== [[File:Homi_Shrine_Torii.JPG|thumb|220px|
Torii
]] The {{ill|lt=hongū|Q11520115|qid=Q11520115|1=hongū}} (okumiya) is near the summit of Nadeshiki-yama (Asahi-yama), elevation 1037 m, and the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=satomiya|1=UNKNOWN}} is on the eastern foot of the mountain. The satomiya area, Kamijō Minamiwari of Nirasaki Asahi-chō, lies on the western edge of the {{ill|lt=Kōfu Basin|Q11577819|qid=Q11577819|1=Kōfu Basin}}, on a terrace along the right bank of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamanashi River|1=UNKNOWN}}, with the {{ill|lt=Midai River|Q11488828|qid=Q11488828|1=Midai River}} flowing south. This area in medieval times belonged to {{ill|lt=Amari-shō|Q11574250|qid=Q11574250|1=Amari-shō}}, and in early modern times Kamijō Minamiwari village was established. Nadeshiki-yama is 1032 m high and is said to have prospered as a mountain of faith in the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}}. The {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|qid=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} deity register records “Homi Shrine” in Koma District. Candidate shrines include this shrine, {{ill|lt=Homi Shrine (Anayama, Nirasaki)|Q11597094|qid=Q11597094|lt=Homi Shrine|1=Homi Shrine}} in Anayama-chō, Nirasaki, and {{ill|lt=Homi Shrine (Minami-Alps)|Q11597095|qid=Q11597095|lt=Homi Shrine|1=Homi Shrine}} in Takao, {{ill|lt=Minami-Alps|Q824000|qid=Q824000|1=Minami-Alps, Yamanashi}}. Formerly called Nadeshikiyama Gongen, and on the summit there is said to have been the {{ill|lt=Shingon|Q550182|qid=Q550182|1=Shingon Buddhism}} temple Nadeshikiyama Hōshō-ji (now abandoned) as {{ill|lt=bettōji|Q11396755|qid=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}}. Remains of the mountain gate, guardian gate, bell tower, and foundations/terraces of guest hall and kitchen have been confirmed, along with excavated artifacts such as {{ill|lt=Haji ware|Q5639938|qid=Q5639938|1=Haji pottery}}, {{ill|lt=ink-inscribed pottery|Q11429419|qid=Q11429419|1=ink-inscribed pottery}}, and {{ill|lt=green-glazed pottery|Q48758368|qid=Q48758368|1=green-glazed pottery}}. According to ''{{ill|lt=Kai no Kuni Shi|Q11577871|qid=Q11577871|1=Kaikokushi}}'', it once had a vast sacred precinct but declined after military damage in the {{ill|lt=Jinshin conflict of Tenshō|Q10939819|qid=Q10939819|1=Tenshō-Jingo war}}. In the Sengoku period it was protected by the Kai shugo {{ill|lt=Takeda clan|Q1210114|qid=Q1210114|1=Takeda clan}}; according to ''Kai no Kuni Shaki/Jiki'', in {{ill|lt=Eiroku|Q1143341|qid=Q1143341|1=Eiroku}} 9 ({{ill|lt=1566|Q6638|qid=Q6638|1=1566}}) it was exempted from munabetsu tax and construction labor. In {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|qid=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 13 (1583), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|qid=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} confirmed its shrine lands. In the early modern period, several halls stood in the precincts, including Kokūzō Hall enshrining {{ill|lt=Kokūzō Bosatsu|Q416174|qid=Q416174|1=Ākāśagarbha}}, the shrine’s honji-butsu. Kokūzō Bosatsu is linked to lake traditions of the Kōfu Basin and to a creation legend that opened the basin, planted seedlings, and taught rice cultivation; worshippers came to pray for crop abundance. The mountain name “Nadeshiki-yama” derives from this creation legend. Along the approach to the okumiya are chō-seki markers set in {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|qid=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1691|Q7682|qid=Q7682|1=1691}}), and at the 13-chō point stands a stone torii from {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|qid=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1664|Q7581|qid=Q7581|1=1664}}). The approach has repeatedly deteriorated and been rerouted over time; later excavations could not locate many markers, especially continuously from 4-chō to 18-chō. The stone torii at 13-chō is now slightly off the present path, and clear traces before and after it are lacking, so the old route through the torii is not well understood. {{ill|lt=Hagiwara Motokatsu|Q6537296|qid=Q6537296|1=Hagiwara Motoe}}’s ''{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kai Meishōshi|1=UNKNOWN}}'' transmits a waka by {{ill|lt=Saigyō|Q224689|qid=Q224689|1=Saigyō}} on the view from Nadeshiki-yama, but ''Kai no Kuni Shi'' rejects it as lacking firm evidence. [[ファイル:十三丁目の石鳥居.jpg|サムネイル|Stone torii at 13-chō on the Nadeshikiyama okumiya approach]] In 2001, remains from ancient to medieval periods were found during forest-road construction near the summit. This led to three academic excavations; especially in the third, large-scale surveys covered the approach from satomiya to okumiya and the summit area. Across the three surveys, Heian-period Haji ware, hearth traces, and pit-house remains were found, showing that rituals were already being conducted around the time of formation of Amari-shō at the mountain foot. However, even these surveys did not newly find remains or artifacts from the 13th-15th centuries (Kamakura-Muromachi), so the condition of the Nadeshiki-yama area including Homi Shrine okumiya in that period remains unclear. Results were compiled in the 2011 ''Comprehensive Academic Survey Report on Nadeshiki-yama''. === Access to the okumiya === From satomiya to okumiya is about 1.5-2 hours one way on foot. Most of the present route is unpaved forest road, with part as mountain trail, so mountaineering-level equipment is recommended. === Notes === === External links === * [https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/maizou-bnk/topics/1-100/0073.html Nadeshikiyama summit site (Yamanashi Prefectural Archaeological Center)] ===References=== * ''Japan Historical Place Names 19: Place Names of Yamanashi Prefecture'', {{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|qid=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}, {{ill|lt=1995|Q2068|qid=Q2068|1=1995}} * ''Comprehensive Study of Nadeshiki-yama: Comprehensive Academic Survey Report on Nadeshiki-yama'', ed. Nadeshikiyama Comprehensive Academic Survey Research Group, {{ill|lt=2011|Q1994|qid=Q1994|1=2011}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Homi Shrine (Asahimachi, Nirasaki)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Homi Shrine (Asahimachi, Nirasaki)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Homi Shrine (Asahimachi, Nirasaki)]][[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kai Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Son-sha in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:韮崎市の歴史]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Nirasaki]]
